elements.sent-2411-002: This paper describes two C++/Open Motion Planning Library implementations of the recently developed motion planning algorithms HyRRT \cite{wang2022rapidly and HySST \cite{10383466 .}}
elements.sent-2411-003: Specifically, cHyRRT, an implementation of the HyRRT algorithm, is capable of generating a solution to a motion planning problem for hybrid systems with probabilistically completeness, while cHySST, an implementation of the asymptotically near-optimal HySST algorithm, is capable of computing a trajectory to solve the optimal motion planning problem for hybrid systems.
elements.sent-2411-004: The structure, components, and usage of the two tools are described.
elements.sent-2411-005: Examples are included to illustrate the main capabilities of the toolbox.
elements.sent-2411-006: Significant progress has been made in the implementation of motion planners for high-dimensional systems in the widely-used Open Motion Planning Library (OMPL) \cite{sucan2012the-open-motion-planning-library .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Several recent additions to the sampling-based motion planning library include Advanced BIT* \cite{strub_icra20a , Space-time RRT* \cite{22-grothe-ICRA , and Task-space RRT \cite{doi:10.1177/0278364919890408 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Such implementations are examples of well-developed motion planning algorithms for purely continuous-time systems and purely discrete-time systems.
elements.sent-2411-009: However, motion planning for hybrid systems, in which the states can evolve continuously and, at times, exhibit jumps, along with implementations of such algorithms, are still open problems.
elements.sent-2411-010: The continuous evolution of $x$ is captured by the flow map $f$.
elements.sent-2411-011: The discrete evolution of $x$ is captured by the jump map $g$.
elements.sent-2411-012: The flow set $C$ collects the points where the state can evolve continuously.
elements.sent-2411-013: The jump set $D$ collects the points where jumps can occur.
elements.sent-2411-014: In this modeling framework, differential and difference equations with constraints are used to describe the continuous and discrete behavior of the hybrid system, respectively; see \cite{goebel2009hybrid for a detailed definition.}
elements.sent-2411-015: This general hybrid system framework can capture most hybrid systems emerging in robotic applications, not only the class of hybrid systems considered in \cite{branicky2003samplingbased , but also systems with timers, impulses, and constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Rapidly-exploring Random Trees (RRT), in practice, as a sampling-based algorithm, is able to compute trajectories for high-dimension systems more efficiently by incrementally constructing a search tree through randomly sampling the state space.
elements.sent-2411-017: Following the RRT algorithm scheme, HyRRT in \cite{wang2022rapidly is proposed to solve the motion planning problem for hybrid systems, inheriting its computation advantages while addressing hybrid systems, which is general to capture purely continuous-time and purely discrete-time systems as well.}
elements.sent-2411-018: At each iteration, HyRRT randomly picks a state sample and extends the search tree by flow or jump, which is also chosen randomly when both regimes are possible.
elements.sent-2411-019: Therefore, the planner takes in flow and jump maps $f$ and $g$ and flow and jump sets $C$ and $D$ representing the system’s dynamics, starting, final, and unsafe state sets, to return an OMPL solution status and OMPL motion plan.
elements.sent-2411-020: Solutions generated by RRT converge to a sub-optimal solution \cite{hysst-7 , while variants of Probabilistic Road Map and RRT, such as PRM* and RRT* \cite{hysst-8 , which solve optimal motion planning problems with guaranteed asymptotic optimality, require a steering function that limits their application.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: On the other hand, the stable sparse RRT (SST) algorithm presented in \cite{hysst-9 does not require a steering function and is guaranteed to be asymptotically near optimal, which means that the probability of finding a solution that has a cost close to the minimal cost converges to one as the number of iterations goes to infinity.}
elements.sent-2411-022: A key advantage of HySST over HyRRT is that, as an SST-type algorithm, HySST generates near-optimal solutions.
elements.sent-2411-023: HySST differs from HyRRT in two aspects: i) HySST takes in the input pruning radius $\delta_{S \in \mathbb{R _{>0 $ to remove all vertices, excluding the vertex with the lowest cost, within $\delta_{S}$ of the static, witness state, and ii) HySST takes in the input selection radius $\delta_{BN \in \mathbb{R _{>0}$ to select the vertex with the lowest cost within $\delta_{BN}$ of the randomly sampled vertex to start propagation from.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: If there are no vertices within the ball defined by radius $\delta_{BN}$, then the nearest vertex is selected.
elements.sent-2411-025: To date, there are no such implementations of motion planners for hybrid dynamical systems in OMPL \cite{sucan2012the-open-motion-planning-library .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Such planners are highly valuable because OMPL not only contains tools for benchmarking, but is also compatible with the widely used Robot Operating System (ROS) \cite{doi:10.1126/scirobotics.abm6074 , both directly and through the most widely-used robotic manipulation software: MoveIt 2\footnote{See https://moveit.ai/ for more information about the MoveIt library , which is also the core ROS 2 robotics manipulation platform.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Compatibility with ROS, which contains libraries and tools for developing, simulating, and visualizing robots, is a notable advantage of both tools.
elements.sent-2411-028: To generate a motion plan for a given hybrid systems, we introduce cHyRRT and cHySST.
elements.sent-2411-029: Both tools consist of functions to define the hybrid system and simulate dynamics, set-checking functions to satisfy both state-space safety and kinodynamic constraints, and a script to visualize the generated trajectories in the ROS 2 visualization package RViz 2.
elements.sent-2411-030: The remainder of the paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-032: Section 3 presents the motion planning problem for hybrid dynamical systems.
elements.sent-2411-033: Section 4 presents the HyRRT algorithm and details of cHyRRT's implementation, usage, and customizations.
elements.sent-2411-034: Section 5 presents the HySST algorithm, including details of cHySST's implementation, usage, and customizations.
elements.sent-2411-035: Section 6 presents example applications of both algorithms and illustrations of the generated motion plans.

elements.section-2411-003: Motion Planning for Hybrid Dynamical Systems
elements.sent-2411-063: This section formulates the motion planning problem for hybrid dynamical systems and introduces the data structure that implements the solution to this problem.
elements.sent-2411-064: This paper solves a motion planning problem for hybrid dynamical systems, defined by flow and jump sets $C$ and $D$, flow and jump maps $f$ and $g$, along with the conditions to i) start from a given initial state, ii) end within a given goal state set, and iii) avoid reaching the unsafe set.
elements.sent-2411-065: This problem is mathematically formulated as follows.
elements.figure-2411-001: \incfig[0.75]{hybrid-motion-planning-problem} \caption{Illustration of a sample motion plan to problem 1, where the solid blue lines denote flow and dotted red lines denote jumps in the motion plan.} \label{fig:1}

elements.sent-2411-082: Our motion planning algorithms search for motion plans by incrementally constructing search trees.
elements.sent-2411-083: The search tree is modeled by a directed tree.
elements.sent-2411-084: A directed tree $\mathcal{T}$ is a pair \( \mathcal{T = (V, E) \), where \( V \) is a set whose elements are called vertices and \( E \) is a set of paired vertices whose elements are called edges.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \incfig[0.6]{motion-plan} \caption{\revised{Illustration of the search tree constructed by HyRRT and HySST. The path \( p = (v_1, v_2, \dots, v_6) \) and the solution pair \( \tilde{e}_p = \tilde{e}_1 | \tilde{e}_2 | \dots | \tilde{e}_5 \).}}
elements.sent-2411-085: The edges in the directed tree are directed, which means the pairs of vertices that represent edges are ordered.
elements.sent-2411-086: The set of edges \( E \) is defined as \( E \subseteq \{(v_1, v_2) : v_1 \in V, v_2 \in V, v_1 \neq v_2\} \).
elements.sent-2411-087: The edge \( e = (v_1, v_2) \in E \) represents an edge from \( v_1 \) to \( v_2 \).
elements.sent-2411-088: A path in \(\mathcal{T = (V, E) \) is a sequence of vertices \( p = (v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k) \) such that \( (v_i, v_{i+1 ) \in E \) for each \( i \in \{ 1, 2, \dots, k - 1 \} \).}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Each vertex in the search tree \( \mathcal{T \) is associated with a state value of $\mathcal{H}$.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Each edge in the search tree is associated with a solution pair to $\mathcal{H}$ that connects the state values associated with their endpoint vertices.
elements.sent-2411-091: The state value associated with vertex \( v \in V \) is denoted as \( x_v \) and the solution pair associated with edge \( e \in E \) is denoted as \( \tilde{e = (\phi, u)\), where \( \phi :\) dom \(\phi \rightarrow \mathbb{R ^n, u : \) dom \(u \in \mathbb{R ^m\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The solution pair that the path \( p = (v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k) \) represents is the concatenation of all those solutions associated with the edges therein, namely, \begin{equation}\tilde{p} := \tilde{e}_{(v_1, v_2)} | \tilde{e}_{(v_2, v_3)} | \dots | \tilde{e}_{(v_{k-1}, v_k)}\end{equation} where \( \tilde{p \) denotes the solution pair associated with the path \( p \).}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{HyRRT algorithm} \label{algorithm:HyRRT} Input: $X_0, X_f, X_u, \mathcal{H} = (C, f, D, g), (U_C, U_D), p \in (0, 1), K \in \mathbb{N} > 0$ \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE $\mathcal{T} \gets \text{init}(X_0)$ \FOR{$k = 1$ to $K$} \STATE \text{randomly select a real number } $r$ \text{ from } $[0, 1]$ \IF{$r \leq p$} \STATE $x_{\text{rand}} \gets \text{random\_state}(C)$ \STATE \text{extend}($\mathcal{T}, x_{\text{rand}}, (U_C, U_D), \mathcal{H}, X_u, \text{flow}$) \ELSE \STATE $x_{\text{rand}} \gets \text{random\_state}(D)$ \STATE \text{extend}($\mathcal{T}, x_{\text{rand}}, (U_C, U_D), \mathcal{H}, X_u, \text{jump}$) \ENDIF \ENDFOR \RETURN$\mathcal{T}$ \end{algorithmic} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Function{extend}{$\mathcal{T}, x, (U_C, U_D), \mathcal{H}, X_u, \text{flag}$} \STATE $v_{\text{cur}} \gets \text{nearest\_neighbor}(x, \mathcal{T}, \mathcal{H}, \text{flag})$ \IF{new state($x, v_{\text{cur}}, (U_C, U_D), \mathcal{H}, X_u, x_{\text{new}}, \tilde{e}_{\text{new}}$)} \STATE $v_{\text{new}} \gets \mathcal{T}.\text{add\_vertex}(x_{\text{new}})$ \STATE$\mathcal{T}.\text{add\_edge}(v_{\text{cur}}, v_{\text{new}}, \tilde{e}_{\text{new}})$ \IF{$x_{\text{new}} == x$} \STATE return Reached \ELSE \STATE return Advanced \ENDIF \ENDIF \STATE return Trapped \end{algorithmic}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{HySST algorithm} \label{algorithm:HySST} Input: $X_0, X_f, X_u, \mathcal{H} = (C, f, D, g), (U_C, U_D), p_n \in (0, 1), K \in \mathbb{N}_{>0}, X_c, X_d, \newline \epsilon_{BN}, \epsilon_s$ \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE $\mathcal{T} \gets \text{init}(X_0)$ \STATE $V_{\text{active}} \gets V$, $V_{\text{inactive}} \gets \emptyset$, $S \gets \emptyset$ \FOR{all $v_0 \in V$} \IF{is\_vertex\_locally\_the best($x_{v_0}$, 0, $S$, $\epsilon_s$)} \STATE .9\linewidth{ $(S, V_{\text{active}}, V_{\text{inactive}}, E) \gets$ \text{prune\_dominated\_vertices}($v_0$, S, $V_{\text{active}}, V_{\text{inactive}}, E$)} \ENDIF \ENDFOR \FOR{$k = 1$ to $K$} \STATE randomly select a real number $r$ from $[0, 1]$ \IF{$r \leq p_n$} \STATE $x_{\text{rand}} \gets \text{random\_state}(C)$ \STATE $v_{\text{cur}} \gets \text{best\_near\_selection}(x_{\text{rand}}, V_{\text{active}}, \epsilon_{BN}, X_c)$ \ELSE \STATE $x_{\text{rand}} \gets \text{random\_state}(D)$ \STATE $v_{\text{cur}} \gets \text{best\_near\_selection}(x_{\text{rand}}, V_{\text{active}}, \epsilon_{BN}, X_d)$ \ENDIF \STATE .9\linewidth{ $(\text{is\_a\_new\_vertex\_generated}, x_{\text{new}}, \tilde{e}_{\text{new}}, \text{cost}_{\text{new}}) \gets$ \hspace*{1.5em} $\text{new\_state}(v_{\text{cur}}, (U_C, U_D), \mathcal{H}, X_u)$} \IF{$\text{is\_a\_new\_vertex\_generated} and$ is\_vertex\_locally\_the\_best($x_{\text{new}}$, $\text{cost}_{\text{new}}$, S, $\epsilon_s$)} \STATE .9\linewidth{ $v_{\text{new}} \gets V_{\text{active}}.\text{add\_vertex}(x_{\text{new}}, \text{cost}_{\text{new}})$ \hspace*{1.5em} $E.\text{add\_edge}(v_{\text{cur}}, v_{\text{new}}, \tilde{e}_{\text{new}})$} \STATE .9\linewidth{ $(S, V_{\text{active}}, V_{\text{inactive}}, E) \gets$ \hspace*{1.5em} $\text{prune\_dominated\_vertices}(v_{\text{new}}, S, V_{\text{active}}, V_{\text{inactive}}, E)$} \ENDIF \ENDFOR \STATE return$ \; \mathcal{T}$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-216: Leveraging the computational efficiency of C++, applicability to high-dimensional, hybrid systems of the RRT-type and SST-type tools, and generalizability of OMPL and ROS to robotics applications, we present two highly versatile motion planning tools.

elements.sent-2411-218: This paper describes two C++/Open Motion Planning Library implementations of the recently developed motion planning algorithms HyRRT \cite{wang2022rapidly and HySST \cite{wang2023hysst .}}
elements.sent-2411-219: In future work, we will implement HyRRT-Connect, a bidirectional RRT algorithm presented in \cite{wang2024hyrrt , in C++/OMPL.}
elements.sent-2411-220: This paper presents two implementations of the recently developed motion planning algorithms HyRRT \cite{wang2022rapidly and HySST \cite{wang2023hysst .}}
elements.sent-2411-221: Specifically, cHyRRT, an implementation of the HyRRT algorithm, generates solutions to motion planning problems for hybrid systems with a probabilistic completeness guarantee, while cHySST, an implementation of the asymptotically near-optimal HySST algorithm, finds near-optimal trajectories based on a user-defined cost function.
elements.sent-2411-222: The implementations align with the theoretical foundations of hybrid system theory and are designed based on OMPL, ensuring compatibility with ROS while prioritizing computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-223: The structure, components, and usage of both tools are detailed.
elements.sent-2411-224: A modified pinball game and collision-resilient tensegrity multicopter example are provided to illustrate the tools' key capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-225: Motion planning is becoming an increasingly important tool for researchers and practitioners as emerging robotic systems such as quadrupled robots and drones enter operation in complex environments.
elements.sent-2411-226: Among various implementations, the Open Motion Planning Library (OMPL) \cite{sucan2012the-open-motion-planning-library is widely used due to its compatibility with the Robot Operating System (ROS) and its comprehensive collection of state-of-the-art planners for systems with purely continuous-time or purely discrete-time dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-227: However, motion planning for hybrid systems, in which the states can evolve continuously and, at times, exhibit jumps, along with implementations of such algorithms, are not currently available.
elements.sent-2411-228: Here, flow map $f$ and jump map $g$ capture continuous and discrete evolution of $x$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-229: This general framework for hybrid systems captures a broad class of hybrid systems, including the class of hybrid systems considered in \cite{branicky2003samplingbased and those that incorporate timers, impulses, constraints, and environmental contacts.}
elements.sent-2411-230: Within this framework, the feasible and the optimal motion planning problems for hybrid dynamical systems are formulated and addressed in \cite{wang2022rapidly and \cite{wang2023hysst , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-231: In practice, Rapidly-exploring Random Trees (RRT)-type algorithms efficiently compute trajectories for high-dimensional problems by incrementally constructing a search tree through random state-space sampling.
elements.sent-2411-232: Following the RRT algorithm scheme, HyRRT in \cite{wang2022rapidly solves the feasible motion planning problem for hybrid systems, inheriting its probabilistically complete guarantee, meaning that the probability of failing to find a motion plan converges to zero as the number of samples approaches infinity \cite{LaValle1998RapidlyexploringRT .}}
elements.sent-2411-233: In many motion planning applications, an optimal solution is preferred over merely a feasible but suboptimal one \cite{hysst-6 .}
elements.sent-2411-234: Unfortunately, solutions generated by RRT may converge to a suboptimal plan \cite{hysst-7 , while optimal variants such as PRM* and RRT* \cite{hysst-8 rely on steering functions, restricting applicability.}}
elements.sent-2411-235: In contrast, the stable sparse RRT (SST) algorithm \cite{hysst-9 eliminates the need for a steering function while guaranteeing asymptotic near-optimality, meaning that as the number of samples approaches infinity, the probability of finding a solution with a cost close to the minimum converges to one.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Therefore, HySST is particularly useful when a user-defined cost functional is provided to evaluate the quality of solutions.
elements.sent-2411-237: HySST differs from HyRRT in two aspects: i) HySST takes in the input pruning radius $\delta_{S \in \mathbb{R _{>0 $ to remove all vertices, excluding the vertex with the lowest cost, within $\delta_{S}$ of the (static) witness state, and ii) HySST takes in the input selection radius $\delta_{BN \in \mathbb{R _{>0}$ to select the vertex with the lowest cost within $\delta_{BN}$ of the randomly sampled vertex to start propagation from.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-238: To date, no motion planners for hybrid dynamical systems have been implemented within OMPL.
elements.sent-2411-239: Compatibility with ROS, which provides libraries and tools for developing, simulating, and visualizing robots, is a key advantage of both tools.
elements.sent-2411-240: However, those features are not available for hybrid dynamical systems.

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{HySST algorithm} \label{algorithm:HySST} Input: $X_0, X_f, X_u, \mathcal{H} = (C, f, D, g), (U_C, U_D), p_n \in (0, 1), K \in \mathbb{N}_{>0}, X_c, X_d, \newline \epsilon_{BN}, \epsilon_s$ \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE $\mathcal{T} \gets init(X_0)$ \STATE $V_{active} \gets V$, $V_{inactive} \gets \emptyset$, $S \gets \emptyset$ \FOR{all $v_0 \in V$} \IF{is\_vertex\_locally\_the best($x_{v_0}$, 0, $S$, $\epsilon_s$)} \STATE .9\linewidth{ $(S, V_{active}, V_{inactive}, E) \gets$ prune\_ dominated\_vertices($v_0$, $S$, $V_{active}$, $V_{inactive}$, $E$)} \ENDIF \ENDFOR \FOR{$k = 1$ to $K$} \STATE randomly select a real number $r$ from $[0, 1]$ \IF{$r \leq p$} \STATE $x_{rand} \gets random\_state(C)$ \STATE $v_{cur} \gets best\_near\_selection(x_{rand}, V_{active}, \newline \epsilon_{BN}, X_c)$ \ELSE \STATE $x_{rand} \gets random\_state(D)$ \STATE $v_{cur} \gets best\_near\_selection(x_{rand}, V_{active}$, $\epsilon_{BN}$, $X_d)$ \ENDIF \STATE .9\linewidth{ $(is\_a\_new\_vertex\_generated$, $x_{new}$, $\tilde{e}_{new}$, $cost_{new}) \gets\begin{equation}new\_state(v_{cur}, (U_C, U_D), H, X_u)$} \IF{$is\_a\_new\_vertex\_generated and$ is\_vertex\_locally\_the\_best($x_{new}$, $cost_{new}$, S, $\epsilon_s$)} \STATE .9\linewidth{ $v_{new} \gets V_{active}.add\_vertex(x_{new}, cost_{new})$ \hspace*{1.5em} $E.add\_edge(v_{cur}, v_{new}, \tilde{e}_{new})$} \STATE .9\linewidth{ $(S, V_{active}, V_{inactive}, E) \gets$ \hspace*{1.5em} $prune\_dominated\_vertices\end{equation}(v_{new}$, $S$, $V_{active}, V_{inactive}, E)$} \ENDIF \ENDFOR \STATE return$ \; \mathcal{T}$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.section-2411-011: Discussion: Evolution of Solution Costs Over Range of HySST Solution Batch Sizes and Limitations
elements.subsection-2411-019: Evolution of Solution Costs Over Range of HySST Solution Batch Sizes
elements.sent-2411-344: The data collected from $15$ runs of cHySST solving the pinball motion planning problem shows an inverse correlation between solution batch size and the cost of the lowest-cost solution.
elements.sent-2411-345: As the batch size increases, both the minimum and mean costs of the $15$ lowest-cost solutions decrease.
elements.sent-2411-346: However, while increasing batch size generally reduces the cost of the generated solution with the best cost, it also increases computational consumption.

elements.sent-2411-352: The two tools cHyRRT and cHySST, for planning of hybrid systems, were described and illustrated in examples.
elements.sent-2411-353: Leveraging the computational efficiency of C++, the applicability to high-dimensional hybrid systems of the RRT-type and SST-type tools, and the generalizability of OMPL and ROS to robotics applications, we present two motion planning tools.
elements.sent-2411-354: In future work, we will implement HyRRT-Connect, a bidirectional RRT algorithm, in C++/OMPL.
